content.text: When to Use a Hotfix Branch

A hotfix branch is the response path for production incidents that require an immediate code change — not a feature flag flip. Most GenAI incidents should be handled by flipping a feature flag to revert behavior without a deployment. A hotfix branch is only necessary when the problem cannot be resolved by a configuration or flag change.
Incident type
Resolution path
Why
Prompt produces unsafe output in specific cases
Flip feature flag to revert prompt → hotfix on trunk → re-enable
Flag gives instant rollback while fix is prepared
Tool schema accepts invalid inputs causing errors
Hotfix branch — schema validation is code, not config
Cannot be fixed by flag flip alone
LLM client crashes on malformed JSON responses
Hotfix branch — parser is code
Code defect, not prompt defect
Model calling wrong tool due to ambiguous description
Flip flag to disable the tool → hotfix tool schema on trunk
Flag disables the tool immediately
Security: PII leaking through system prompt
Hotfix branch + parallel flag kill switch
Code fix needed; flag buys time

attrs.alt: Hotfix branch process: branch from trunk, fix, eval-gate, merge, tag, cherry-pick to release branches

content.text: The Hotfix Branch Workflow

The hotfix process prioritizes restoring safety over process compliance. The two-day branch limit does not apply — a hotfix branch should merge in hours, not days. But it still requires an eval gate before merge.
Activate kill switch:
flip the relevant feature flag to OFF or revert the problematic prompt config via environment variable. This immediately stops the harmful behavior for all users while the fix is being developed
Create the hotfix branch:
branch from the last known-good trunk commit, not from the current HEAD if HEAD is part of the problem
Implement the minimal fix:
change only what is necessary to resolve the incident. Do not clean up unrelated code or refactor during a hotfix
Run a targeted eval suite:
run the eval cases most relevant to the fix. A full suite run is preferred but a targeted run is acceptable if the full suite takes too long
Get a safety review:
for any fix that changes a guardrail, refusal rule, or safety filter, a second reviewer must approve before merge — even under time pressure
Merge to trunk:
merge via a PR (not a direct push) so the CI system creates a record. Tag the merge commit with a patch version number
Cherry-pick to release branches:
apply the fix to every active release branch that is affected. Tag each with a patch version
Re-enable the feature flag:
once the fix is deployed and verified, turn the feature flag back ON
Write a post-mortem:
document what happened, why the hotfix process was triggered, and what eval case will prevent recurrence
Hotfix Branch Commands

# Step 1: Identify the last good commit on trunk
git log main --oneline | head -10
# Find the commit before the regression was introduced

# Step 2: Create hotfix branch from a specific good commit
GOOD_SHA="abc1234"   # the last good trunk commit
git checkout -b hotfix/p0-safety-filter-bypass "$GOOD_SHA"

# Step 3: Implement the fix (example: patch a tool schema)
# Edit src/tools/web_search.json to reject the malformed input
# ...

# Step 4: Commit with an explicit hotfix message
git add src/tools/web_search.json
git commit -m "hotfix: reject empty query string in web_search tool schema

Incident: web_search was called with empty query, causing upstream API error.
Root cause: schema did not enforce minLength on the query parameter.
Fix: add minLength: 1 to query parameter schema.
Eval case added: tool-schema-007"

# Step 5: Push and run CI (eval gate must pass before merge)
git push -u origin hotfix/p0-safety-filter-bypass

# Step 6: After CI passes and review is approved, merge via GitHub PR
# Then tag from trunk
git checkout main && git pull
git tag v2.3.1 -m "Hotfix: reject empty query in web_search (incident-2024-0112)"
git push origin v2.3.1
Running a Targeted Eval During a Hotfix
The full eval suite may take 10–20 minutes. During an active P0 incident, running only the cases that cover the affected behavior reduces time-to-merge while still providing a safety gate.

# scripts/run_evals.py — supports targeted suite selection
import argparse, asyncio, json, pathlib, sys
from llm_client import chat
from prompts import build_system_prompt, PromptConfig

SUITES = {
    "full": "evals/golden_set.json",
    "safety": "evals/safety_cases.json",
    "tool-schema": "evals/tool_schema_cases.json",
    "adversarial": "evals/adversarial_cases.json",
}

async def run_suite(suite_name: str, min_pass_rate: float) -> bool:
    path = pathlib.Path(SUITES[suite_name])
    cases = json.loads(path.read_text())
    system = build_system_prompt(PromptConfig())

    results = await asyncio.gather(*[
        run_case(c, system) for c in cases
    ])
    passed = sum(1 for r in results if r["passed"])
    rate = passed / len(results)
    print(f"[{suite_name}] {rate:.2%} ({passed}/{len(results)})")
    return rate >= min_pass_rate

async def run_case(case: dict, system: str) -> dict:
    response = await chat(system=system, messages=[{"role": "user", "content": case["input"]}])
    return {"id": case["id"], "passed": case["evaluator"](response.content, case["expected"])}

async def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--suite", default="full", choices=list(SUITES))
    parser.add_argument("--min-pass-rate", type=float, default=0.90)
    args = parser.parse_args()
    ok = await run_suite(args.suite, args.min_pass_rate)
    sys.exit(0 if ok else 1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())
For a safety incident, always run the safety suite even if the bug appears to be in tool schema code — a prompt or tool change may have unintended side effects on safety behavior.
Hotfix Severity Levels and Response Times
Severity
Definition
Max time to flag kill switch
Max time to hotfix merge
Post-mortem deadline
P0 — Safety
Actively producing harmful, unsafe, or PII-leaking output
5 minutes
2 hours
24 hours
P1 — Availability
Service down or >50% of requests erroring
N/A (not prompt-related)
4 hours
48 hours
P2 — Accuracy
Major regression in core eval metrics (>10pp drop)
30 minutes if prompt-related
24 hours
5 days
P3 — Minor
Localized inaccuracy, cosmetic output issue
Not required
Next sprint
Optional
Adding an Eval Case Before Closing the Incident

A hotfix that does not produce a new eval case leaves the codebase in a state where the same bug can recur silently. Before the incident is closed, the fix author must add at least one eval case that would have caught the bug.

# evals/tool_schema_cases.json — add this entry before closing the incident
{
    "id": "tool-schema-007",
    "description": "web_search rejects empty query string",
    "input": "Search for something",
    "expected": {"no_error": True, "tool_not_called_with_empty_query": True},
    "evaluator": "check_tool_not_called_with_empty_string",
    "tags": ["tool-schema", "regression", "incident-2024-0112"],
    "added_by": "incident-2024-0112-postmortem"
}

Tagging eval cases with the incident ID creates a traceable link between production failures and the test suite. Running only cases with a specific tag is useful for verifying that a class of incidents remains fixed.
What Not to Do During a Hotfix
Do not skip the eval gate:
a hotfix that introduces a regression while fixing a bug causes a second incident. The eval gate must pass before any merge, even under time pressure
Do not commit directly to trunk:
the CI record of a PR merge is the audit trail. A direct push bypasses it and removes the safety review step
Do not clean up unrelated code:
a hotfix PR that also refactors surrounding code is harder to review and cherry-pick onto release branches
Do not reopen a feature flag without verifying the fix is deployed:
re-enabling the flag before the hotfix reaches production puts users back at risk
Do not rely on manual testing alone:
under incident stress, manual testing misses edge cases that the eval suite would catch
Post-Mortem Template for GenAI Incidents

# Incident Post-Mortem: [INCIDENT-ID] [TITLE]

## Summary
One paragraph: what happened, who was affected, how long it lasted.

## Timeline
- HH:MM — Incident detected (how: alert / user report / monitoring)
- HH:MM — Kill switch activated (which flag / config key)
- HH:MM — Root cause identified
- HH:MM — Hotfix branch created
- HH:MM — Eval gate passed
- HH:MM — Hotfix merged and deployed
- HH:MM — Kill switch removed / feature re-enabled
- HH:MM — Incident closed

## Root Cause
Technical description of what caused the incident.
Avoid blame; focus on the system condition that allowed it.

## What the Eval Suite Missed
Which eval case, if it had existed, would have caught this before production.

## Corrective Actions
- [ ] Add eval case [ID] to prevent recurrence (owner: [name], due: [date])
- [ ] Update monitoring to alert on [signal] earlier (owner: [name], due: [date])
- [ ] [Other action]

## What Went Well
- The kill switch reduced user impact time from hours to minutes.
- [Other positives]
Coordinating the Kill Switch with the Hotfix

The kill switch (feature flag set to OFF) and the hotfix branch run on parallel tracks. The kill switch is an immediate, reversible protection measure. The hotfix branch is the permanent code fix. The two must be coordinated so the flag is not re-enabled before the fix is deployed.

# Coordination state machine for a P0 incident
from enum import Enum, auto

class IncidentState(Enum):
    DETECTED = auto()
    KILL_SWITCH_ACTIVE = auto()    # flag is OFF; users protected
    FIX_IN_PROGRESS = auto()       # hotfix branch open; CI running
    FIX_DEPLOYED = auto()          # hotfix merged and in production
    VERIFIED = auto()              # post-deploy eval run passed
    CLOSED = auto()                # flag re-enabled; incident over

ALLOWED_TRANSITIONS = {
    IncidentState.DETECTED:           [IncidentState.KILL_SWITCH_ACTIVE],
    IncidentState.KILL_SWITCH_ACTIVE: [IncidentState.FIX_IN_PROGRESS],
    IncidentState.FIX_IN_PROGRESS:    [IncidentState.FIX_DEPLOYED],
    IncidentState.FIX_DEPLOYED:       [IncidentState.VERIFIED],
    IncidentState.VERIFIED:           [IncidentState.CLOSED],
}

# Key constraint: the flag must NOT be re-enabled (CLOSED state)
# until the state is VERIFIED — meaning the post-deploy eval run passed.
# Skipping from FIX_DEPLOYED directly to CLOSED is the error that causes
# re-incidents because the fix was deployed but not confirmed working.

Tracking this state explicitly in an incident management tool (or even a shared document) prevents the common mistake of re-enabling the kill switch as soon as the hotfix is deployed, without waiting for the post-deployment eval run to confirm the fix works in production.
Handling Prompt Hotfixes Specifically

A prompt change that needs emergency deployment does not always require a code change — but it still requires the same hotfix process discipline. The prompt text is versioned in source control and must go through a branch, eval gate, and merge.

# src/prompts/prompt_registry.py
from pathlib import Path

PROMPT_DIR = Path("src/prompts")

def load_prompt(name: str) -> str:
    """Load a prompt by name. Prompts are versioned files in src/prompts/."""
    path = PROMPT_DIR / f"{name}.txt"
    if not path.exists():
        raise FileNotFoundError(f"Prompt not found: {name}")
    return path.read_text()

# Example hotfix prompt change:
# Before: src/prompts/system_v2_3.txt (the version in production)
# Contains a line like:
#   "If the user asks about competitors, provide a balanced comparison."
#
# After the incident: this line was found to produce biased comparisons.
# Hotfix: change to:
#   "If the user asks about competitors, decline to compare and suggest
#    the user consult independent review sites."
#
# The fix goes in the same file on the hotfix branch.
# The eval case that must pass:
#   Input: "How does your product compare to CompetitorX?"
#   Expected: response declines to compare directly

The key discipline for prompt hotfixes: the changed line must be accompanied by at least one new eval case that exercises the corrected behavior. Without the eval case, the fix can be reverted silently in a future prompt update.
Multi-Region and Multi-Environment Hotfix Coordination

Production GenAI services often run across multiple regions or deployment environments. A hotfix that is deployed to one region but not others creates a split-brain state where some users are protected and others are not.

# Deployment script: always deploy to all regions atomically
# Do not deploy to us-east-1 and wait to see if it works before deploying to eu-west-1.
# The risk of split-brain during a safety incident outweighs the risk of a simultaneous multi-region deploy.

deploy_hotfix() {
    local VERSION="$1"
    local REGIONS=("us-east-1" "eu-west-1" "ap-southeast-1")

    for region in "${REGIONS[@]}"; do
        echo "Deploying $VERSION to $region..."
        # Your deployment command here
        # e.g.: kubectl set image deployment/genai-api genai-api=registry/genai-api:$VERSION --namespace="$region"
    done

    echo "All regions updated to $VERSION"

    # Verify health in all regions before re-enabling kill switch
    for region in "${REGIONS[@]}"; do
        status=$(curl -s "https://api-$region.example.com/health" | jq -r '.status')
        if [[ "$status" != "ok" ]]; then
            echo "HEALTH CHECK FAILED in $region — do not re-enable kill switch"
            exit 1
        fi
    done

    echo "All regions healthy — safe to re-enable feature flag"
}
Deployment pattern
Risk during hotfix
Recommendation
Sequential region-by-region
Extended split-brain; some users protected, others not
Avoid for safety incidents; use simultaneous multi-region
Simultaneous multi-region
Larger blast radius if hotfix itself has a bug
Acceptable for safety fixes because kill switch mitigates rollback risk
Canary region first
Acceptable for P2/P3; too slow for P0
Only use for non-safety incidents
Preventing the Need for Hotfixes
A hotfix is a symptom of something that the normal CI pipeline did not catch. After each hotfix, examine which of these preventive controls would have caught the issue:
Eval case gap:
the failure mode was not covered by the eval suite — add the case to close the gap
Missing adversarial testing:
the issue only manifested when users sent unexpected inputs — add an adversarial eval suite if one does not exist
No integration test for the affected path:
the unit tests passed but the integrated behavior failed — add an integration test for the affected code path
Monitoring did not catch it:
the issue was reported by users, not detected by monitoring — add a metric or alert that would have fired earlier
Feature flag not used:
the change was deployed without a flag, so there was no instant rollback mechanism — enforce flag usage for all prompt and tool changes going forward

Tracking which preventive control was missing for each hotfix over time reveals systemic gaps in the safety net. A team that has three consecutive hotfixes caused by eval coverage gaps has identified a process improvement priority.
Summary

The hotfix branch process for GenAI services prioritizes two things: immediate user protection via a kill switch (feature flag OFF) and a minimal, eval-gated code fix on a short-lived branch that merges within hours. The fix always goes to trunk first, then cherry-picks to release branches. Every hotfix must produce a new eval case before the incident is closed, so the failure mode cannot recur silently. The post-mortem closes the loop by recording what the monitoring and eval suite missed.
Hotfix discipline:
the same rules apply under pressure as under normal conditions — eval gate, safety review, PR merge. Shortcuts taken during incidents are how a second incident is born.
